title: Latent race-regime state from pit-wave and neutralization signals
summary: Pit timing changes abruptly under safety-car windows, low-grip warm-up conditions, or sudden field-wide pit waves.
rationale: Pit timing changes abruptly under safety-car windows, low-grip warm-up conditions, or sudden field-wide pit waves. Official F1 coverage explicitly mentions safety-car windows and warm-up-sensitive undercut/overcut balance. A latent state model can capture these regime shifts better than static handcrafted flags.
implementation_hint: Create race-lap aggregate signals first: number of pitters this lap and previous 1-3 laps, field mean/std of lap delta, field mean/std of degradation, fraction on each compound, and abrupt shifts in these aggregates. Fit a small HMM/change-point model per race or globally on these aggregates, output posterior regime probabilities for each row, and feed those posteriors into the booster.
expected_effect: Could improve recall on clustered pit events and unusual laps where many drivers react to a common exogenous state rather than individual tyre wear.
risk: If inferred states are unstable across folds or mostly recapitulate race progress, the extra complexity will not pay off. Keep the state space very small.
